item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially perplexed by the containment procedures, but I did slowly get a grasp of it and it seemed interesting to me. Once I got to the part where it described its telepathy, I got exceptionally curious. This lead to the collapsible message which really made me feel warm and fuzzy and tied everything together - something about it seemed very wholesome to me.

Overall, a very good skip in my opinion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is certainly an interesting scip. While there isn't much to it, I still found it to be a really cool "explaination to strange natural phenomenon"-type anomaly.
(Funny enough, it also kinda reminds me of the "Hunger Tube" short from Cyanide and Happiness, except with penguins and a giant Antarctic worm.)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Alright, so, since SCP-2296's effects also appear within relevant instances of SCP-2720, it would likely be reasonable for Johnny Foundation to see if the effects also are replicated with Emperor Penguins produced from SCP-646 within a controlled environment, as that gives some insight into what exactly the force behind 2296 may be. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-2296 is also observed within relevant SCP-2720 iterations.

To this:

SCP-2296 is also observed within relevant SCP-2720 iterations, as well as within relevant populations generated from SCP-646.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On one hand, this is about penguins and I love penguins and we don't have enough of them on the site.

On the other, it's an anomalous explanation of a magnificent natural process… done in a rather boring way. "Weird tubes feed the penguins and keep them warm" is not particularly interesting. The feel-good aura is okay, but not enough to truly invest me in the skip.

Sorry, but -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes. Which in real life is part of why they huddle:
This is actual Antarctic weather- coastal Antarctica is basically in an perpetual windstorm of varying magnitude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
200 km/h winds

Wouldn't the penguins be blown away in such high winds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: gosh darn penguin maximizers!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And it's not just that it's an alien consciousness that we can't fathom, it's literally a horror, something hellbent on ending all sentient life, or humanity, or whatever… Just not as much as it's concerned with protecting penguins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object classes refer to difficulty of containment, and considering these things manifest seasonally, rather than just existing year round, it's very hard to get them into a box and call it safe.

I'm pretty sure that, in order to maintain the air of "what is this?" that this piece has, both a conclusive DNA trace and a successful radar probe would ruin it. So, you're left with an inconclusive DNA test, and these things just stretching into the Earth's core. Neither of those things add anything to the piece, so leaving them out is optimal.

At least, that's my viewpoint on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral. This anomaly is just too weird for the Foundation to just say "hands off, everyone." some testing ideas :

  • DNA analysis of the nutrient to try to identify the content
  • deep radar to trace the origin of the tubes

And why Euclid? The only thing that would be harmed if this was uncontained would be penguins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea of some alien horror being utterly concerned with the well-being of penguins is such a good and pure idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These penguins unfortunately can't afford the rates at Awashima Hotel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's weird to think that emperor penguins are only able to survive their breeding season thanks to an anomaly. This starts to push the line of "what is anomalous", vis-a-vis anomalous species.

That said, I was reminded of that one skip where a hotel room opens into a penguin breeding ground. I'd like to think these are related and something out there is really concerned with making sure penguins are protected. Final addendum got my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That collapsible is really unnecessary, considering that it holds so little information and isn't disrupting the flow of anything. Otherwise, it's a cute article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Technically it's actually summer that's coming at the south pole. X3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ths removes the great wonder of how emperor penguins survive in such situations but I'm okay with.

And given that the vocalizations affect any living being, I'm guessing Gaia loves its penguins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: trudat, I have too many footnotes as is

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article makes my inner penguin feel warm and safe, especially since Winter is coming. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think footnote 4 should be moved into the main body of the description, but take my upbote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big bundles of thanks to Lex1nat0r, minmin, and ghostpage for their help over on IRC.

Image credit (modified by myself)

(I wonder if I can keep up with posting one article a week…)

I added two more images on 2018-07-08.

First: cropped screenshot of a video accompanying an academic paper. The paper was published in an open-access journal. I credit the authors of this paper: Zitterbart D, Wienecke B, Butler J, and Fabry B.

Second: CC0 photo found on Pixabay.
